blocks.content: Which means you didn't have time to decide not to.
The words sat in the quiet of his room long after the screen went dark.

Ethan looked at the ceiling. The same ceiling he'd stared at for three years in this apartment. But his mind wasn't here anymore — it was in a different room, a different ceiling. Older. Textured plaster with a water stain shaped like something he used to make into a map.
He was nine. Maybe ten.

The house went from warm to hollow between one breath and the next. That was the thing about it. No warning. His parents' voices below — not the words, he'd learned not to listen to words. Words could be taken back. But tone didn't lie. And the tone that night had the specific sound of something ending, the particular frequency of two people who had stopped trying to sound like people who weren't breaking.

He'd gone back to his room. Closed the door softly — even then, some part of him understood that making himself small was the safest option. Sat on the edge of his bed the same way he was sitting now. And decided something he wouldn't have been able to name at the time, something his body understood before his mind did:
Don't give anything the chance to matter that much.

It became the architecture of him. The way he moved through rooms without leaving marks. The way he kept friendships at exactly the temperature where they were warm but couldn't burn anything. The way he painted the world at a careful distance — observed, rendered, cataloged. Never absorbed.

Nathan had seen through it from the start. That was the infuriating thing about Nathan — he had the particular gift of people who are entirely themselves, which is that they recognize when someone else isn't. He'd said it once, junior year, after Ethan had turned down the fourth invitation in a row for reasons that kept sounding different and were all the same reason:
"You love everything you won't let yourself love."
Ethan had told him to mind his business.
Nathan had laughed. "That IS my business. I'm your business."
Ethan almost smiled at the memory. Almost.
His phone buzzed.
You still there?
Yeah.
You went somewhere.
Just thinking.
About her?

About her. About every reason not to. About the way those reasons felt thinner tonight than they had in years — like he'd been carrying something he'd told himself was load-bearing, and someone had simply pointed out that the house was still standing without it.
About a lot of things.
She worth it?
Ethan looked across the room at Gerald. The duck stared back — unqualified, unbothered, completely unhelpful.
I think so.
Then stop thinking.
She sounds like the kind of person you meet once.

Ethan felt the weight of that settle somewhere quiet. Because he knew. He'd known it from the moment she stopped in front of his painting and started talking to him like he was someone worth talking to. The kind of knowing that doesn't ask for confirmation.
Yeah.
Then show up tomorrow.
Don't think about it. Just show up.
Ethan set the phone down. Lay back. The fountain sketch was on his desk — he could see it from here. The figure near the easel, barely suggested. Not erased.
He thought about what she'd said.
Trying is underrated.
He closed his eyes. And for the first time in a long time, he let tomorrow feel like something worth moving toward.
